skill.name: gitlab-mr-issue
skill.description: Guide AI assistants through GitLab CLI workflows for viewing, creating, and updating merge requests and issues.
skill.summary: Use GitLab CLI commands to manage merge requests and issues with consistent team-friendly descriptions.
skill.author: DCjanus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command patterns, but they are Markdown examples for legitimate GitLab CLI workflows. The confirmed risk is that the skill directs agents to run authenticated glab, git, and jq commands that can read and modify remote GitLab project data.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitLab Operations Can Read And Modify Project Data
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to use glab commands for viewing issues, viewing MR diffs, adding notes, creating merge requests, creating issues, and updating existing records. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can expose repository metadata and change remote GitLab state when run with an authenticated account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly document authenticated glab read and write operations. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Examples Require Careful Parameter Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill includes shell examples with command substitution and heredoc descriptions. The examples are normal CLI usage, but agents should avoid inserting untrusted titles, labels, branch names, or descriptions without preserving quoting and non-interactive safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution patterns are present and legitimate. Injection risk depends on how future user-provided values are inserted into these examples.

low_findings.title: Static High-Severity Pattern Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, high entropy, and path traversal alerts do not correspond to executable cryptographic code, packed content, or filesystem traversal. The flagged text is Markdown documentation, Chinese prose, and placeholder ellipses in command examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found Markdown front matter, command examples, and placeholders at the flagged locations. No executable crypto routine, binary blob, or filesystem traversal logic is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is built around running glab, git, and jq commands. These commands should be executed only in the intended repository and GitLab project context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and central to the skill. They are not hidden or obfuscated, but they require normal command-execution caution.

content.value_statement: GitLab issue and merge request work often needs consistent commands and clear descriptions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical GitLab CLI workflow for viewing, creating, and updating those records.
actual_capabilities: Checks GitLab CLI authentication and active account details.
Views GitLab issues, comments, merge requests, and merge request diffs.
Creates non-interactive merge requests with structured titles and descriptions.
Creates GitLab issues with labels, assignees, due dates, and confidentiality options.
Updates existing issue or merge request titles and descriptions after reading current content.
limitations: Requires glab to be installed and authenticated on the local machine.
Requires correct GitLab host and project context for self-hosted instances.
Does not validate team policy beyond the written guidance in the skill.
Can modify remote GitLab records when used with write-capable credentials.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Merge Request
use_cases.description: Create a non-interactive GitLab merge request with a clear summary, key changes, constraints, and testing notes.

use_cases.title: Review Issue Context
use_cases.description: Open a GitLab issue with comments and system logs before planning implementation work.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Updates
use_cases.description: Update issue or merge request titles and descriptions after checking the current content.

prompt_templates.title: View an Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to view GitLab issue 123 with comments, then summarize the current problem and requested outcome.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Merge Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect merge request 45, including its description and diff. Summarize the main change and review risks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Merge Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a non-interactive GitLab merge request from the current branch to main with a concise semantic title.

prompt_templates.title: Update Existing Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to read the current merge request description, then update it with clearer summary, testing, and release notes.

output_examples.output: The assistant reports the issue title, current status, key discussion points, and any unresolved questions.

output_examples.input: Create a merge request for the current branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates the merge request non-interactively and returns the complete GitLab URL on its own line.

output_examples.input: Update an existing merge request description.
output_examples.output: The assistant first reads the current description, then replaces it with a clearer summary, key changes, constraints, and testing notes.

best_practices: Confirm the GitLab host, project, and authenticated user before making changes.
Read the current issue or merge request content before updating it.
Use clear titles, concise descriptions, and explicit testing notes.
anti_patterns: Do not run write commands against an unclear GitLab project.
Do not overwrite existing descriptions without reading them first.
Do not paste secrets or private tokens into issue or merge request text.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitLab CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on the glab command-line tool and a valid authenticated GitLab session.

faq.question: Can it work with self-hosted GitLab?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes using GITLAB_HOST or repository options for self-hosted GitLab instances.

faq.question: Can it create merge requests without an editor?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses non-interactive command patterns with titles, descriptions, branches, labels, and confirmation flags.

faq.question: Can it update existing issues or merge requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs the assistant to read current content before changing a title or description.

faq.question: Does it review merge request code?
faq.answer: It can fetch merge request diffs for inspection, but it does not provide a complete review framework by itself.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The skill can run authenticated commands that read or change remote GitLab data, so users should confirm project context first.
